task_text:
Fix `StringRef` comparison not being `constexpr` when possible

In `internal/catch_stringref.hpp`, the `operator==` for `StringRef` performs a length check followed by `std::memcmp`, but neither is marked `constexpr`. Since C++17 allows `constexpr` string operations, the comparison should be `constexpr` for compile-time test name deduplication.
solve_notes: Marked `operator==` and `operator!=` as `constexpr` and replaced `std::memcmp` — which is not `constexpr` in C++17 — with a simple pointer index loop that compares characters one by one. Removed the now-unnecessary `#include <cstring>` from the header (the `.cpp` file retains its own `<cstring>` include for `std::strlen` in the `char const*` constructor).

search_sequence.action: Read operator== at line 59 of catch_stringref.hpp
search_sequence.result: Uses `std::memcmp(m_start, other.m_start, m_size)` — std::memcmp is not constexpr in C++17/20 standard; not marked constexpr
search_sequence.reasoning: Task description pointed directly to this operator in this file

search_sequence.action: Read m_start and m_size field declarations at lines 34-35 of catch_stringref.hpp
search_sequence.result: m_start is `char const*`, m_size is `size_type` (size_t); both accessible for a direct pointer index loop
search_sequence.reasoning: Needed to confirm field types and accessibility before writing the constexpr loop

search_sequence.result: Included solely for std::memcmp; grep of the rest of the header shows no other cstring functions
search_sequence.reasoning: Once memcmp is removed the include becomes unused and should be removed

search_sequence.action: Read catch_stringref.cpp to verify it has its own #include <cstring>
search_sequence.result: File has `#include <cstring>` at line 12 and uses `std::strlen` in the char const* constructor at line 16
search_sequence.reasoning: Confirmed removing cstring from the header is safe — the cpp's own include is sufficient

key_decisions.decision: Use an index loop over m_start rather than __builtin_memcmp or std::equal
key_decisions.reasoning: The index loop is standard C++11 constexpr-compatible, portable across all compilers Catch2 supports, and requires no extra includes

exploration_log.aha_moment: Confirming that the `.cpp` file has its own `#include <cstring>` made it safe to remove the header's include without risking breakage elsewhere that transitively depends on stringref.hpp.
exploration_log.hindsight: Could have checked catch_stringref.cpp first to verify the cstring usage before touching the header include.

minimum_sufficient_defs.name: StringRef::operator==
minimum_sufficient_defs.reason: edited: marked constexpr and replaced std::memcmp with a constexpr character index loop

minimum_sufficient_defs.name: StringRef::operator!=
minimum_sufficient_defs.reason: edited: marked constexpr (delegation to operator== makes this trivially constexpr once operator== is)

thrash_preventing_defs.name: StringRef
thrash_preventing_defs.reason: read: class declaration showing m_start (char const*) and m_size (size_type) fields is needed to write the index loop; an agent without this would search for field declarations

thrash_preventing_defs.name: StringRef::StringRef
thrash_preventing_defs.reason: read: confirmed the cpp file has its own #include <cstring> for strlen, making it safe to remove cstring from the header; without this an agent might leave the include out of caution or keep it

tier_difference_reasoning: minimum_sufficient is the two edited operators. thrash_preventing adds the class declaration (so field names and types are known without searching) and the .cpp constructor (to confirm the cstring include can be safely dropped from the header). An agent without the class declaration would always open it anyway; without the .cpp confirmation it would keep the unused include.

queries.query_text: A string view type used throughout the test framework cannot be compared at compile time because its equality operator relies on a runtime memory comparison function that is not allowed in constant expressions
queries.justification: Rule compliance: uses domain terms 'string view type', 'compile time', 'equality operator', 'runtime memory comparison', 'constant expressions' — no symbol names. Pre-implementation: a developer wanting constexpr string comparison would describe the issue this way.

Rule compliance: 'std::memcmp' is the literal function call in the source that grep finds. Pre-implementation: a developer looking for the non-constexpr comparison would search for memcmp in the stringref implementation.
Rule compliance: five exact symbol names from the diff and the class. Pre-implementation: a developer working on StringRef equality would identify these names first.

queries.query_text: methods of StringRef that are not constexpr
queries.justification: Rule compliance: names concrete symbol 'StringRef' and uses relationship word 'methods'. Pre-implementation: a developer auditing StringRef for constexpr opportunities would ask which methods are missing the qualifier.
Rule compliance: two explicit file paths from the solution. Pre-implementation: a developer fixing StringRef comparison would navigate to the header and implementation.

queries.query_text: Make StringRef operator== constexpr by replacing the std::memcmp call with a portable character loop
queries.justification: Rule compliance: mixes domain concept 'constexpr', 'portable character loop' with exact symbols 'StringRef' and 'operator=='. Pre-implementation: a developer who has identified the target function would frame the task this way.
Rule compliance: two symbol names plus one file path. Pre-implementation: a developer who has located the header would target both comparison operators by name.

queries.query_text: Mark StringRef::operator== constexpr in catch_stringref.hpp: replace std::memcmp with a constexpr-compatible index loop and remove the unused <cstring> include
queries.justification: Rule compliance: includes symbols, file path, and specific action items. Pre-implementation: this is the query a developer would write once they have identified the file and the function to fix.

test_selection.test_query: Find tests that verify the behavior of StringRef::operator== in src/catch2/internal/catch_stringref.hpp

relevant_preexisting_tests.reason: Contains 'Comparisons are deep' section at line 91 that directly calls operator== on two StringRefs with different pointer addresses but equal content
